title: MarginalizationAK
tex_block_html:
Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $W \ins V$ a subset of output nodes. Then the marginalization of $G$ w.r.t.\ $W$ or the latent projection of $G$ onto $J\cup V \sm W$ is the CDMG: \[ G^{V\sm W | J} := G^{\sm W} := (J^{\sm W},V^{\sm W},E^{\sm W},L^{\sm W}), \]

where:

  1. $J^{\sm W}:= J$,
  2. $V^{\sm W}:= V \sm W$,
  3. $E^{\sm W}$ consists of all directed edges $\ul{v} \tuh \ol{v}$ with $\ul{v},\ol{v} \in J \cup V \sm W$ for which there exists a directed walk in $G$: \[\ul{v} \tuh w_1 \tuh \cdots \tuh w_{n-1} \tuh \ol{v}, \] where all intermediate nodes $w_1,\dots,w_{n-1} \in W$ (if any).
  4. $L^{\sm W}$ consists of all bidirected edges $\ul{v} \huh \ol{v}$ with $\ul{v},\ol{v} \in V \sm W$, $\ul{v} \neq \ol{v}$, for which there exists a bifurcation in $G$: \[\ul{v} \hut w_1 \hut \cdots \hut w_{k-1} \hus w_k \tuh \cdots \tuh w_{n-1} \tuh \ol{v}, \] where all intermediate nodes $w_1,\dots,w_{n-1} \in W$ (if any).

tex_statement.raw:
% Definition file for row def_3_14 -- "MarginalizationAK".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solve_chapter.py at row start. The
% manager agent (or a worker dispatched by it) fills the body below with the
% Lean-faithful definition statement(s). Stays close to the lecture notes.
%
% This file is a sibling of `main.tex` inside `<subsection>/tex/`. The
% `subfiles` package lets it compile both standalone (resolving its
% preamble via `main.tex`) and as part of `main.tex` via `\subfile{...}`.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: def_3_14
% title: MarginalizationAK
\def\rowref{def\_3\_14}
\phantomsection\label{def_3_14}

\begin{Def}[Marginalization a.k.a.\ latent projection on CDMGs]\label{def:G_marginalization}
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}) and $W \ins V$ a subset of output nodes. The \emph{marginalization of $G$ w.r.t.\ $W$}, equivalently the \emph{latent projection of $G$ onto $J \cup (V \sm W)$}, is the CDMG
    \[
        G^{V \sm W \,|\, J} \;:=\; G^{\sm W} \;:=\; \lp J^{\sm W},\, V^{\sm W},\, E^{\sm W},\, L^{\sm W} \rp,
    \]
    whose four components are defined as follows.
    \begin{enumerate}[label=\roman*.)]
        \item \emph{Input nodes.}
            \[ J^{\sm W} \;:=\; J. \]

        \item \emph{Output nodes.}
            \[ V^{\sm W} \;:=\; V \sm W. \]

        \item \emph{Directed edges.} The directed-edge set is the set of pairs witnessed by a directed walk in $G$ whose intermediate vertices lie in $W$:
            \[
                E^{\sm W} \;:=\; \lC (\ul{v}, \ol{v}) \in \lp J \cup (V \sm W) \rp \times (V \sm W) \st \Phi_E(\ul{v}, \ol{v}) \rC,
            \]
            where for $\ul{v} \in J \cup (V \sm W)$ and $\ol{v} \in V \sm W$ the directed-walk-through-$W$ predicate $\Phi_E(\ul{v}, \ol{v})$ holds if and only if there exist an integer $n \ge 1$ and a tuple $(w_0, w_1, \ldots, w_n) \in (J \cup V)^{n+1}$ such that all of the following hold:
            \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
                \item \emph{End-points.} $w_0 = \ul{v}$ and $w_n = \ol{v}$.
                \item \emph{Intermediates lie in $W$.} $w_i \in W$ for every $i \in \{1, \ldots, n - 1\}$ (vacuous when $n = 1$).
                \item \emph{Consecutive $E$-edges.} $(w_{i-1}, w_i) \in E$ for every $i \in \{1, \ldots, n\}$.
            \end{enumerate}
            In words: $\Phi_E(\ul{v}, \ol{v})$ asserts the existence of a directed walk in $G$ from $\ul{v}$ to $\ol{v}$, in the sense of def \ref{def:walks}, item~ii., of length $n \ge 1$, all of whose intermediate vertices $w_1, \ldots, w_{n-1}$ (if any) lie in $W$.

            \emph{No $\ul{v} \neq \ol{v}$ constraint.} The condition $\ul{v} \neq \ol{v}$ is \emph{not} imposed in the set-builder for $E^{\sm W}$. Self-cycles $(v, v) \in E^{\sm W}$ are admitted exactly under the witness conditions above, namely via a directed walk through $W$ that returns to $v$.\footnote{This may introduce self-cycles into $E^{\sm W}$.}

        \item \emph{Bidirected edges.} The bidirected-edge set is the set of distinct pairs witnessed by a bifurcation in $G$ whose intermediate vertices lie in $W$:
            \[
                L^{\sm W} \;:=\; \lC (\ul{v}, \ol{v}) \in (V \sm W) \times (V \sm W) \st \ul{v} \neq \ol{v} \,\land\, \Phi_L(\ul{v}, \ol{v}) \rC,
            \]
            where for $\ul{v}, \ol{v} \in V \sm W$ the bifurcation-through-$W$ predicate $\Phi_L(\ul{v}, \ol{v})$ holds if and only if there exist an integer $n \ge 1$, an index $k \in \{1, \ldots, n\}$, and a tuple $(w_0, w_1, \ldots, w_n) \in (J \cup V)^{n+1}$ such that the data $\bigl((w_0, w_1, \ldots, w_n),\, k\bigr)$ describes a bifurcation between $\ul{v}$ and $\ol{v}$ in $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def:walks}, item~vi., \emph{and} all intermediate vertices $w_1, \ldots, w_{n-1}$ (if any, i.e.\ when $n \ge 2$) lie in $W$. Unfolding def \ref{def:walks}, item~vi., at the present row, $\Phi_L(\ul{v}, \ol{v})$ is the conjunction of:
            \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
                \item \emph{End-points, intermediates, and walk length.} $w_0 = \ul{v}$, $w_n = \ol{v}$, $n \ge 1$, $k \in \{1, \ldots, n\}$, and $w_i \in W$ for every $i \in \{1, \ldots, n-1\}$ (vacuous when $n = 1$).
                \item \emph{End-nodes appear exactly once.} $w_0 \notin \lC w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n \rC$ and $w_n \notin \lC w_0, w_1, \ldots, w_{n-1} \rC$. (Under the constraints of (a) this is automatic, since $\ul{v}, \ol{v} \in V \sm W$, while $w_1, \ldots, w_{n-1} \in W$ and $\ul{v} \neq \ol{v}$ is enforced by the outer set-builder.)
                \item \emph{Left directed arm} (vacuous when $k = 1$). For every $i \in \{1, \ldots, k - 1\}$,
                    \[ (w_i, w_{i-1}) \in E. \]
                    (Equivalently, in def \ref{not-cdmg} notation, $w_{i-1} \hut w_i$ in $G$ for every such $i$.)
                \item \emph{Central hinge} at index $k$ --- either a directed-fork hinge or a bidirected hinge:
                    \[ (w_k, w_{k-1}) \in E \;\;\text{or}\;\; (w_{k-1}, w_k) \in L. \]
                    (Equivalently, $w_{k-1} \hus w_k$ in $G$, in def \ref{not-cdmg} notation.)
                \item \emph{Right directed arm} (vacuous when $k = n$). For every $i \in \{k, \ldots, n - 1\}$,
                    \[ (w_i, w_{i+1}) \in E. \]
                    (Equivalently, $w_i \tuh w_{i+1}$ in $G$ for every such $i$.)
                \item \emph{End-node arrowhead constraint} (inherited from def \ref{def:walks}, item~vi., clause~(e)). When $k = n$, only the \emph{bidirected} alternative of the hinge in (d) is admitted, namely $(w_{k-1}, w_k) \in L$; the directed alternative $(w_k, w_{k-1}) \in E$ at $k = n$ is \emph{excluded}, because it would place the only arrowhead incident to $w_n$ at $w_{n-1}$ rather than at $w_n = \ol{v}$, contradicting clause~(e) of def \ref{def:walks}, item~vi. For every $k$ with $1 \le k \le n - 1$, both alternatives in (d) are admitted (the right directed arm of (e) supplies an arrowhead at $w_n$ regardless of the hinge type, and the right arm also gives $w_k$ at least one out-edge so the directed-fork hinge becomes a genuine fork with source $w_k$). Symmetrically at $w_0$: for $k \ge 2$ the left arm supplies an arrowhead at $w_0$ via $(w_1, w_0) \in E$; for $k = 1$ both hinge alternatives are edges into $w_0$ (the directed alternative $(w_1, w_0) \in E$ puts an arrowhead at $w_0$, and the bidirected alternative $(w_0, w_1) \in L$ puts arrowheads at both ends), so neither is excluded at $k = 1$.
            \end{enumerate}

            \emph{Boundary cases authorised by the conventions $w_0 := \ul{v}$, $w_n := \ol{v}$ and $k \in \{1, \ldots, n\}$.} The conventions explicitly extend the displayed index range of the bifurcation walk to its endpoints, so that all of the following configurations qualify as bifurcation-through-$W$ witnesses for $L^{\sm W}$, provided $\ul{v} \neq \ol{v}$ and every intermediate $w_i$ (for $1 \le i \le n - 1$) lies in $W$:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item $n = 1$, $k = 1$ (both arms vacuous; $k = n$, so clause~(f) admits only the bidirected hinge): the witness reduces to the single hinge edge $(w_0, w_1) = (\ul{v}, \ol{v}) \in L$ --- i.e., a direct bidirected edge $\ul{v} \huh \ol{v}$ already present in $G$ between non-marginalised nodes, with no intermediates. The directed-hinge alternative is excluded here by clause~(f).
                \item $n = 2$, $k = 1$ (left arm vacuous): one intermediate $w_1 \in W$; the hinge at index $k = 1$ is $(w_1, \ul{v}) \in E$ (the ``Y''-fork case with $w_1$ as source) or $(\ul{v}, w_1) \in L$ (the bidirected-hinge case); the right directed arm contributes $(w_1, \ol{v}) \in E$.
                \item $n = 2$, $k = n = 2$ (right arm vacuous; $k = n$, so clause~(f) admits only the bidirected hinge): one intermediate $w_1 \in W$; the left directed arm contributes $(w_1, \ul{v}) \in E$; the hinge admits only the bidirected alternative $(w_1, \ol{v}) \in L$ (the mirror ``Y'' with bidirected edge into $\ol{v}$).
            \end{itemize}
            Larger values of $n$ and $k$ are also admitted, with the same structural skeleton: a left arm of directed edges all pointing toward $\ul{v}$, a single hinge (directed-fork or bidirected, subject to clause~(f)), a right arm of directed edges all pointing toward $\ol{v}$, and all intermediate vertices in $W$.
    \end{enumerate}

    \emph{Asymmetry between (iii) and (iv) regarding self-edges (intentional; to be preserved by any formalisation).}
    The constraint $\ul{v} \neq \ol{v}$ is imposed in clause (iv) on $L^{\sm W}$ but is \emph{not} imposed in clause (iii) on $E^{\sm W}$. Consequently:
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Directed self-cycles $v \tuh v$ may appear in $E^{\sm W}$ when witnessed by a directed walk through $W$ that returns to $v$.
        \item Bidirected self-edges $v \huh v$ are \emph{excluded} from $L^{\sm W}$ outright by the $\ul{v} \neq \ol{v}$ constraint, irrespective of any bifurcation-through-$W$ that might otherwise witness one.
    \end{itemize}
    Any formalisation of this definition shall preserve this asymmetry exactly: no $\ul{v} \neq \ol{v}$ constraint is to be imposed on $E^{\sm W}$, and no relaxation of the $\ul{v} \neq \ol{v}$ constraint is to be made on $L^{\sm W}$.
\end{Def}

\end{document}
tex_statement.html:
Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg) and $W \ins V$ a subset of output nodes. The marginalization of $G$ w.r.t.\ $W$, equivalently the latent projection of $G$ onto $J \cup (V \sm W)$, is the CDMG \[ G^{V \sm W \,|\, J} \;:=\; G^{\sm W} \;:=\; \lp J^{\sm W},\, V^{\sm W},\, E^{\sm W},\, L^{\sm W} \rp, \] whose four components are defined as follows.

  1. Input nodes. \[ J^{\sm W} \;:=\; J. \]
  2. Output nodes. \[ V^{\sm W} \;:=\; V \sm W. \]
  3. Directed edges. The directed-edge set is the set of pairs witnessed by a directed walk in $G$ whose intermediate vertices lie in $W$: \[ E^{\sm W} \;:=\; \lC (\ul{v}, \ol{v}) \in \lp J \cup (V \sm W) \rp \times (V \sm W) \st \Phi_E(\ul{v}, \ol{v}) \rC, \] where for $\ul{v} \in J \cup (V \sm W)$ and $\ol{v} \in V \sm W$ the directed-walk-through-$W$ predicate $\Phi_E(\ul{v}, \ol{v})$ holds if and only if there exist an integer $n \ge 1$ and a tuple $(w_0, w_1, \ldots, w_n) \in (J \cup V)^{n+1}$ such that all of the following hold:
    1. End-points. $w_0 = \ul{v}$ and $w_n = \ol{v}$.
    2. Intermediates lie in $W$. $w_i \in W$ for every $i \in \{1, \ldots, n - 1\}$ (vacuous when $n = 1$).
    3. Consecutive $E$-edges. $(w_{i-1}, w_i) \in E$ for every $i \in \{1, \ldots, n\}$.

    In words: $\Phi_E(\ul{v}, \ol{v})$ asserts the existence of a directed walk in $G$ from $\ul{v}$ to $\ol{v}$, in the sense of def def:walks, item ii., of length $n \ge 1$, all of whose intermediate vertices $w_1, \ldots, w_{n-1}$ (if any) lie in $W$.

    No $\ul{v} \neq \ol{v}$ constraint. The condition $\ul{v} \neq \ol{v}$ is not imposed in the set-builder for $E^{\sm W}$. Self-cycles $(v, v) \in E^{\sm W}$ are admitted exactly under the witness conditions above, namely via a directed walk through $W$ that returns to $v$.

  4. Bidirected edges. The bidirected-edge set is the set of distinct pairs witnessed by a bifurcation in $G$ whose intermediate vertices lie in $W$: \[ L^{\sm W} \;:=\; \lC (\ul{v}, \ol{v}) \in (V \sm W) \times (V \sm W) \st \ul{v} \neq \ol{v} \,\land\, \Phi_L(\ul{v}, \ol{v}) \rC, \] where for $\ul{v}, \ol{v} \in V \sm W$ the bifurcation-through-$W$ predicate $\Phi_L(\ul{v}, \ol{v})$ holds if and only if there exist an integer $n \ge 1$, an index $k \in \{1, \ldots, n\}$, and a tuple $(w_0, w_1, \ldots, w_n) \in (J \cup V)^{n+1}$ such that the data $\bigl((w_0, w_1, \ldots, w_n),\, k\bigr)$ describes a bifurcation between $\ul{v}$ and $\ol{v}$ in $G$ in the sense of def def:walks, item vi., and all intermediate vertices $w_1, \ldots, w_{n-1}$ (if any, i.e.\ when $n \ge 2$) lie in $W$. Unfolding def def:walks, item vi., at the present row, $\Phi_L(\ul{v}, \ol{v})$ is the conjunction of:

    1. End-points, intermediates, and walk length. $w_0 = \ul{v}$, $w_n = \ol{v}$, $n \ge 1$, $k \in \{1, \ldots, n\}$, and $w_i \in W$ for every $i \in \{1, \ldots, n-1\}$ (vacuous when $n = 1$).
    2. End-nodes appear exactly once. $w_0 \notin \lC w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n \rC$ and $w_n \notin \lC w_0, w_1, \ldots, w_{n-1} \rC$. (Under the constraints of (a) this is automatic, since $\ul{v}, \ol{v} \in V \sm W$, while $w_1, \ldots, w_{n-1} \in W$ and $\ul{v} \neq \ol{v}$ is enforced by the outer set-builder.)
    3. Left directed arm (vacuous when $k = 1$). For every $i \in \{1, \ldots, k - 1\}$, \[ (w_i, w_{i-1}) \in E. \] (Equivalently, in def not-cdmg notation, $w_{i-1} \hut w_i$ in $G$ for every such $i$.)
    4. Central hinge at index $k$ — either a directed-fork hinge or a bidirected hinge: \[ (w_k, w_{k-1}) \in E \;\;\text{or}\;\; (w_{k-1}, w_k) \in L. \] (Equivalently, $w_{k-1} \hus w_k$ in $G$, in def not-cdmg notation.)
    5. Right directed arm (vacuous when $k = n$). For every $i \in \{k, \ldots, n - 1\}$, \[ (w_i, w_{i+1}) \in E. \] (Equivalently, $w_i \tuh w_{i+1}$ in $G$ for every such $i$.)
    6. End-node arrowhead constraint (inherited from def def:walks, item vi., clause (e)). When $k = n$, only the bidirected alternative of the hinge in (d) is admitted, namely $(w_{k-1}, w_k) \in L$; the directed alternative $(w_k, w_{k-1}) \in E$ at $k = n$ is excluded, because it would place the only arrowhead incident to $w_n$ at $w_{n-1}$ rather than at $w_n = \ol{v}$, contradicting clause (e) of def def:walks, item vi. For every $k$ with $1 \le k \le n - 1$, both alternatives in (d) are admitted (the right directed arm of (e) supplies an arrowhead at $w_n$ regardless of the hinge type, and the right arm also gives $w_k$ at least one out-edge so the directed-fork hinge becomes a genuine fork with source $w_k$). Symmetrically at $w_0$: for $k \ge 2$ the left arm supplies an arrowhead at $w_0$ via $(w_1, w_0) \in E$; for $k = 1$ both hinge alternatives are edges into $w_0$ (the directed alternative $(w_1, w_0) \in E$ puts an arrowhead at $w_0$, and the bidirected alternative $(w_0, w_1) \in L$ puts arrowheads at both ends), so neither is excluded at $k = 1$.

    Boundary cases authorised by the conventions $w_0 := \ul{v}$, $w_n := \ol{v}$ and $k \in \{1, \ldots, n\}$. The conventions explicitly extend the displayed index range of the bifurcation walk to its endpoints, so that all of the following configurations qualify as bifurcation-through-$W$ witnesses for $L^{\sm W}$, provided $\ul{v} \neq \ol{v}$ and every intermediate $w_i$ (for $1 \le i \le n - 1$) lies in $W$:

    • $n = 1$, $k = 1$ (both arms vacuous; $k = n$, so clause (f) admits only the bidirected hinge): the witness reduces to the single hinge edge $(w_0, w_1) = (\ul{v}, \ol{v}) \in L$ — i.e., a direct bidirected edge $\ul{v} \huh \ol{v}$ already present in $G$ between non-marginalised nodes, with no intermediates. The directed-hinge alternative is excluded here by clause (f).
    • $n = 2$, $k = 1$ (left arm vacuous): one intermediate $w_1 \in W$; the hinge at index $k = 1$ is $(w_1, \ul{v}) \in E$ (the “Y”-fork case with $w_1$ as source) or $(\ul{v}, w_1) \in L$ (the bidirected-hinge case); the right directed arm contributes $(w_1, \ol{v}) \in E$.
    • $n = 2$, $k = n = 2$ (right arm vacuous; $k = n$, so clause (f) admits only the bidirected hinge): one intermediate $w_1 \in W$; the left directed arm contributes $(w_1, \ul{v}) \in E$; the hinge admits only the bidirected alternative $(w_1, \ol{v}) \in L$ (the mirror “Y” with bidirected edge into $\ol{v}$).

    Larger values of $n$ and $k$ are also admitted, with the same structural skeleton: a left arm of directed edges all pointing toward $\ul{v}$, a single hinge (directed-fork or bidirected, subject to clause (f)), a right arm of directed edges all pointing toward $\ol{v}$, and all intermediate vertices in $W$.

Asymmetry between (iii) and (iv) regarding self-edges (intentional; to be preserved by any formalisation). The constraint $\ul{v} \neq \ol{v}$ is imposed in clause (iv) on $L^{\sm W}$ but is not imposed in clause (iii) on $E^{\sm W}$. Consequently:

  • Directed self-cycles $v \tuh v$ may appear in $E^{\sm W}$ when witnessed by a directed walk through $W$ that returns to $v$.
  • Bidirected self-edges $v \huh v$ are excluded from $L^{\sm W}$ outright by the $\ul{v} \neq \ol{v}$ constraint, irrespective of any bifurcation-through-$W$ that might otherwise witness one.

Any formalisation of this definition shall preserve this asymmetry exactly: no $\ul{v} \neq \ol{v}$ constraint is to be imposed on $E^{\sm W}$, and no relaxation of the $\ul{v} \neq \ol{v}$ constraint is to be made on $L^{\sm W}$.
tex_statement.env_title: Marginalization a.k.a.\ latent projection on CDMGs

lean_blocks.explanation: This is a `variable` declaration: Lean auto-binds `Node : Type*` and the instance `[DecidableEq Node]` as implicit arguments to every subsequent declaration in the surrounding `CDMG` namespace, sparing us from rewriting the same parameters on `marginalize` and its helpers. The `Type*` notation makes `Node` universe-polymorphic, so callers can instantiate it at any universe level. The `DecidableEq Node` assumption is load-bearing for the marginalization construction in particular: the body uses `Finset.product` (`×ˢ`) over `Node × Node` to materialise the LN's product carriers, `Finset.filter` on the resulting `Finset (Node × Node)` with the predicates `MarginalizationΦE` and `e.1 ≠ e.2 ∧ MarginalizationΦL`, and `Finset.sdiff` (`G.V \ W`) — all of which need decidable equality on the element type. Decidable equality also discharges the `e.1 ≠ e.2` clause inside the `L^{∖W}` filter. Stronger structure on `Node` (e.g.\ `Fintype`, `LinearOrder`) is not required here and is deferred to downstream consumers that actually use it. Same convention as the rest of `Chapter3_GraphTheory`'s files.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Auto-binds `Node` + `DecidableEq Node` as implicits; decidability drives `Finset.product`/`filter`/`sdiff` and the `e.1 ≠ e.2` test below.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.code:
def MarginalizationΦE (G : CDMG Node) (W : Finset Node)
    (u v : Node) : Prop :=
  ∃ (p : Walk G u v),
    p.IsDirectedWalk ∧
    p.length ≥ 1 ∧
    (∀ x ∈ p.vertices.tail.dropLast, x ∈ W)
lean_blocks.explanation: `MarginalizationΦE G W u v` is the directed-walk-through-$W$ predicate witnessing membership of $(u, v)$ in the marginalised directed-edge set $E^{\sm W}$. It asserts the existence of a `Walk G u v` (the post-refactor `Walk` inductive from `def_3_4`, whose endpoints are type indices and whose `cons` carries a `WalkStep` constructor for the channel) such that three conjuncts hold: the walk is directed (`p.IsDirectedWalk`, i.e.\ every `WalkStep` is the `forwardE` constructor), has length at least one (so the trivial `Walk.nil` does not vacuously witness reflexive pairs), and every intermediate vertex lies in $W$. The slice $w_1, \ldots, w_{n-1}$ is read off the walk's vertex list via `p.vertices.tail.dropLast` — the standard chapter-3 idiom (`def_3_4`'s `IsBifurcation` uses the same pair). Factored out as a named top-level `def` so every downstream marginalization claim unfolds membership in $(G.\texttt{marginalize}\,W).E$ as a single rewrite, rather than dragging the three-conjunct existential through every proof.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- The directed-walk-through-`W` predicate: $(u, v) \in E^{\sm W}$ iff a witnessing walk exists.
def MarginalizationΦE (G : CDMG Node) (W : Finset Node)
    (u v : Node) : Prop :=
  -- Walk witness from `u` to `v` in `G`; post-refactor `Walk` indexes endpoints at the type level.
  ∃ (p : Walk G u v),
    -- Every `WalkStep` in `p` is the `forwardE` constructor (no `backwardE`, no `bidir`).
    p.IsDirectedWalk ∧
    -- Excludes the trivial `Walk.nil`, which would silently admit every reflexive pair.
    p.length ≥ 1 ∧
    -- Intermediate vertices $w_1, \ldots, w_{n-1}$ (tail drops $w_0$, dropLast drops $w_n$) all lie in `W`.
    (∀ x ∈ p.vertices.tail.dropLast, x ∈ W)

lean_blocks.code:
def MarginalizationΦL (G : CDMG Node) (W : Finset Node)
    (u v : Node) : Prop :=
  (∃ (p : Walk G u v),
      p.IsBifurcation ∧ ∀ x ∈ p.vertices.tail.dropLast, x ∈ W) ∨
  (∃ (p : Walk G v u),
      p.IsBifurcation ∧ ∀ x ∈ p.vertices.tail.dropLast, x ∈ W)
lean_blocks.explanation: `MarginalizationΦL G W u v` is the bifurcation-through-$W$ predicate witnessing the unordered pair $\{u, v\}$ in the marginalised bidirected-edge set $L^{\sm W}$. It asserts the existence of a `Walk` (in either orientation — `Walk G u v` or `Walk G v u`) carrying `p.IsBifurcation` from `def_3_4` and whose intermediate vertices all lie in $W$. `Walk.IsBifurcation` already encodes the LN's clause (a)–(f) bifurcation structure: $u \neq v$, end-nodes-appear-exactly-once, a split index packaging the left-arm/hinge/right-arm shape, and the clause-(e) end-node arrowhead constraint that restricts the $k = n$ hinge to bidirected only. Re-deriving any of this inline would duplicate `def_3_4`'s structural recursion. The disjunction over the two walk orientations is preserved post-refactor even though the new `CDMG` carries no `hL_symm` axiom (symmetry is definitional via `Sym2`): it keeps $\Phi_L$ semantically symmetric for downstream consumers (`claim_3_16` through `claim_3_19`) and matches the LN's undirected "bifurcation between $\underline{v}$ and $\overline{v}$" phrasing.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- The bifurcation-through-`W` predicate: $\{u, v\} \in L^{\sm W}$ iff a witnessing bifurcation exists.
def MarginalizationΦL (G : CDMG Node) (W : Finset Node)
    (u v : Node) : Prop :=
  -- First orientation: walk from `u` to `v` carrying the bifurcation structure of `def_3_4` item vi.
  (∃ (p : Walk G u v),
      -- `IsBifurcation` packages the LN's clause (a)–(f): split index, hinge, arrowhead constraint.
      p.IsBifurcation ∧ ∀ x ∈ p.vertices.tail.dropLast, x ∈ W) ∨
  -- Second orientation: makes $\Phi_L$ evidently symmetric via `Or.comm` (legacy of pre-refactor `hL_symm`).
  (∃ (p : Walk G v u),
      p.IsBifurcation ∧ ∀ x ∈ p.vertices.tail.dropLast, x ∈ W)

lean_blocks.code:
noncomputable def marginalize (G : CDMG Node) (W : Finset Node)
    (hW : W ⊆ G.V) : CDMG Node where
  J := G.J
  V := G.V \ W
  hJV_disj := marginalize_hJV_disj G W
  E := ((G.J ∪ (G.V \ W)) ×ˢ (G.V \ W)).filter
        (fun e => G.MarginalizationΦE W e.1 e.2)
  hE_subset := marginalize_hE_subset G W
  L := (((G.V \ W) ×ˢ (G.V \ W)).filter
        (fun e => e.1 ≠ e.2 ∧ G.MarginalizationΦL W e.1 e.2)).image
        (fun e => s(e.1, e.2))
  hL_subset := marginalize_hL_subset G W
  hL_irrefl := marginalize_hL_irrefl G W
lean_blocks.explanation: `marginalize` is the main definition: given a CDMG `G` and a subset `W ⊆ G.V` of output nodes, it returns the CDMG $G^{\sm W}$. The four data fields read directly from the LN: $J^{\sm W} = G.J$, $V^{\sm W} = G.V \setminus W$, $E^{\sm W}$ is the filter of the product carrier $(G.J \cup (G.V \setminus W)) \times (G.V \setminus W)$ by `MarginalizationΦE`, and $L^{\sm W}$ is the filter of $(G.V \setminus W) \times (G.V \setminus W)$ by `e.1 ≠ e.2 ∧ MarginalizationΦL` post-composed with `Finset.image (fun e => s(e.1, e.2))` to lift into the post-refactor `Finset (Sym2 Node)` carrier. Both ordered pairs $(u, v)$ and $(v, u)$ in the source filter collapse to the same `s(u, v)` in the image, matching the unordered-pair semantics. The four proof obligations (`hJV_disj`, `hE_subset`, `hL_subset`, `hL_irrefl`) are factored out as private lemmas above, keeping the def body pure data plus named-lemma references. `noncomputable` is inherited from the classical decidability instances on $\Phi_E$ and $\Phi_L$.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- `noncomputable` propagates from `Classical.propDecidable` instances on `MarginalizationΦE`/`Φ_L`.
noncomputable def marginalize (G : CDMG Node) (W : Finset Node)
    -- `hW` carried for LN-fidelity ("Let $W \subseteq V$"); not consumed by any axiom discharge.
    (hW : W ⊆ G.V) : CDMG Node where
  -- Input nodes unchanged: $J^{\sm W} := J$ (LN item i).
  J := G.J
  -- Output nodes with `W` removed: $V^{\sm W} := V \setminus W$ (LN item ii).
  V := G.V \ W
  -- Disjointness of `J` and `V \ W` inherited from `G.hJV_disj` since `V \ W ⊆ V`.
  hJV_disj := marginalize_hJV_disj G W
  -- Directed edges $E^{\sm W}$: filter the product carrier by the directed-walk-through-`W` predicate.
  E := ((G.J ∪ (G.V \ W)) ×ˢ (G.V \ W)).filter
        -- No `e.1 ≠ e.2` conjunct: self-cycles admitted per LN footnote / addition.
        (fun e => G.MarginalizationΦE W e.1 e.2)
  -- Carrier-membership obligation reads off `Finset.mem_filter` + `Finset.mem_product`.
  hE_subset := marginalize_hE_subset G W
  -- Bidirected edges $L^{\sm W}$: filter on ordered pairs first, then quotient to `Sym2` via `image`.
  L := (((G.V \ W) ×ˢ (G.V \ W)).filter
        -- Explicit `e.1 ≠ e.2` is the load-bearing discharge of `hL_irrefl` (no bidirected self-edges).
        (fun e => e.1 ≠ e.2 ∧ G.MarginalizationΦL W e.1 e.2)).image
        -- `s(_, _)` is `Sym2.mk` notation; collapses $(u,v)$ and $(v,u)$ to the same unordered pair.
        (fun e => s(e.1, e.2))
  -- `Sym2.Mem`-shaped: every node in the unordered pair lies in `V \ W`.
  hL_subset := marginalize_hL_subset G W
  -- `¬ s.IsDiag` via `Sym2.mk_isDiag_iff` pulled back to the filter's `e.1 ≠ e.2` conjunct.
  hL_irrefl := marginalize_hL_irrefl G W

addition_to_the_LN:
[bifurcation_index_boundary_excludes_natural_cases] The "bifurcation" in (iv) is to be interpreted according to the previously formalized definition of bifurcation, not by the literal index range $w_1, \dots, w_{n-1}$ displayed in the walk. In particular, with the convention $w_0 := \ul{v}$ and $w_n := \ol{v}$, the bidirected-edge position index $k$ ranges over $\{1, \dots, n\}$, so that $n=1$ (a direct bidirected edge $\ul{v} \huh \ol{v}$ already present in $L$ with no intermediates), $n=2, k=1$ (a "Y" $\ul{v} \hus w_1 \tuh \ol{v}$), and $n=2, k=n$ (the mirror "Y" $\ul{v} \hut w_1 \hus \ol{v}$) all qualify as bifurcations witnessing membership in $L^{\sm W}$, provided the intermediate nodes (if any) lie in $W$.

[self_cycle_asymmetry_between_directed_and_bidirected] The asymmetry between clauses (iii) and (iv) regarding self-edges is intentional and shall be preserved by any formalization: directed self-cycles $v \tuh v$ may appear in $E^{\sm W}$ (as flagged by the footnote), while bidirected self-edges $v \huh v$ are excluded from $L^{\sm W}$ by the explicit $\ul{v} \neq \ol{v}$ constraint in clause (iv). A formalization should neither impose $\ul{v} \neq \ol{v}$ on $E^{\sm W}$ nor relax it on $L^{\sm W}$.
design_choices:
**The output is a plain `def`, not a wrapping `structure`, `inductive`, or `class`.** Marginalization is a function `CDMG Node → Finset Node → … → CDMG Node` — new data is not introduced, and the property is not typeclass-resolvable. The CDMG already has its `structure` from `def_3_1`; this row simply produces a new CDMG from an existing one. The same pattern is shared by every chapter-3 section-3.2 CDMG operator (`def_3_10` `HardInterventionOn`, `def_3_11` `NodeSplittingOn`, `def_3_12` `NodeSplittingHard`, `def_3_13` `ExtendingCDMGsWith`).

**The carrier of the result is `Node`, not a tagged-sum carrier.** Unlike `def_3_11` and `def_3_13`, which *create* new nodes and therefore live in `CDMG (SplitNode Node)` / `CDMG (IntExtNode Node)`, marginalization only *removes* nodes ($V \setminus W$); every node of $G^{\sm W}$ already inhabits the original `Node` carrier. This matches `def_3_10`'s shape and keeps the iterated-marginalization equality of `claim_3_17` and the marginalization-vs-intervention identity of `claim_3_18` syntactically clean.

**The precondition `hW : W ⊆ G.V` is an explicit argument, not an instance or a `Finset (Subtype G.V)`.** Subset facts are not generally typeclass-resolvable, so pushing `hW` into an `instance` would force every caller to discharge the membership manually. A `Finset (Subtype G.V)` encoding was rejected because it would force a `Subtype.val` lift at every use site (every intermediate vertex of a witnessing walk, every product-carrier membership check) with no compensating clarity gain — the LN itself writes "$W \subseteq V$". The four proof obligations of `def_3_1`'s post-refactor `CDMG` structure all close without consuming `hW`; the binder is carried purely for LN-faithfulness, with `set_option linter.unusedVariables false in` suppressing the resulting warning (same convention as `def_3_10`).

**Edge sets use `Finset.filter` over the product carrier, with classical decidability.** The LN writes $E^{\sm W}$ and $L^{\sm W}$ as set-builders ranging over $(J \cup (V \setminus W)) \times (V \setminus W)$ and $(V \setminus W)^2$ respectively, filtered by $\Phi_E$ and $\Phi_L$. `Finset.filter` is the closest Lean primitive; the product carrier `_ ×ˢ _` materialises the LN's "ranging over" range, and `Finset.mem_filter` matches the LN's elementhood reading directly. A `Set (Node × Node)` encoding was rejected because `def_3_1` requires `Finset`-backed edges. A constructive reachability fixpoint was rejected because it would add a substantial chunk of new chapter-3 infrastructure for no downstream gain: the marginalization claims reason about *which pairs* lie in $E^{\sm W}$ / $L^{\sm W}$ set-theoretically, not by *running* a decision procedure.

**`L^{∖W}` is built via `(filter …).image (fun e => s(e.1, e.2))`, lifting an ordered-pair filter into the `Sym2`-quotient carrier.** Post-`cdmg_typed_edges`, `def_3_1`'s `L` field has type `Finset (Sym2 Node)` rather than the pre-refactor `Finset (Node × Node)`. Filtering directly over a `Sym2`-carrier would require either a pre-existing `Sym2`-carrier `Finset` to filter from (which we do not have — the LN's $L^{\sm W}$ is set-builder-defined, not derived from `G.L`) or hand-building such a carrier via a more elaborate `Sym2`-equivalence-class step. The filter-then-image pattern is the cleanest LN-faithful encoding: the ordered-pair filter mirrors the LN's set-builder $\{(\underline{v}, \overline{v}) \in (V \setminus W)^2 \mid \underline{v} \neq \overline{v} \land \Phi_L\}$ literally (so the `e.1 ≠ e.2` conjunct stays writable), and `Finset.image (fun e => s(e.1, e.2))` is the standard Mathlib idiom for quotienting an ordered-pair `Finset` to its `Sym2` image. Both ordered pairs $(u, v)$ and $(v, u)$ in the source filter collapse to the same `s(u, v)` in the image, mirroring the unordered-pair semantics.

**The walk-through-$W$ predicates $\Phi_E$ and $\Phi_L$ are factored out as named top-level `def`s.** Inlining them inside the edge-field bodies would force every downstream claim that unfolds membership in $(G.\texttt{marginalize}\,W).E$ or $\ldots.L$ to drag the three-conjunct existential (resp.\ the two-orientation disjunction) through every proof, with ad-hoc destructuring at each site. Named predicates carry the unfolding step as a single rewrite plus dot-notation API, matching the membership-predicate naming convention of every sibling chapter-3 CDMG operator.

**$\Phi_E$ reuses `Walk G u v` rather than a bespoke `List Node` enumeration.** The chapter has already paid the foundational cost of `def_3_4`'s post-refactor `Walk` inductive, whose endpoints are type indices and whose `cons` carries a `WalkStep` constructor (`forwardE`, `backwardE`, `bidir`) for the channel rather than an ordered pair. Walk-algebra lemmas (`IsDirectedWalk`, vertex-membership, concatenation) lift uniformly from the chapter's walks framework. A bespoke `List Node` reformulation would force every downstream marginalization claim to translate back to the `Walk` API.

**$\Phi_E$'s witness must have `p.length ≥ 1`.** Without this conjunct the trivial `Walk.nil v hv : Walk G v v` (vacuously a directed walk) would witness $\Phi_E(v, v)$ for every $v \in J \cup V$, silently admitting every reflexive pair into $E^{\sm W}$ regardless of whether $G.E$ contains the self-edge. The LN's clause (iii) requires $n \ge 1$ explicitly.

**No $u = v \to p.\texttt{length} \ge 2$ self-cycle restriction.** The LN's clause (iii) ranges uniformly over $u = v$ and $u \neq v$ cases, and the footnote "Note that this may introduce self-cycles" explicitly welcomes self-cycles. Imposing a length-$\ge 2$ branch on the $u = v$ case would break two LN-clean properties: (i) $G^{\sm \emptyset} = G$ as identity-on-data — with $W = \emptyset$, no length-$\ge 2$ walk through $W$ exists, so direct self-edges would silently drop on trivial marginalisation; and (ii) `claim_3_17`'s commutation triple equality would admit counterexamples, e.g.\ $G = (\emptyset, \{v, w\}, \{(v, w), (w, v)\}, \emptyset)$ with $W_1 = \{w\}$, $W_2 = \emptyset$.

**$\Phi_L$ is symmetrised via an `Or` over both walk orientations, even though the post-refactor `CDMG` carries no `hL_symm` axiom.** A bifurcation between $u$ and $v$ is semantically symmetric, but the witness type `Walk G u v` is directed — `Walk G u v` and `Walk G v u` are distinct types. Under the previous encoding, this symmetric `Or` was load-bearing for discharging the now-removed `hL_symm` field via `Or.comm`. Under `cdmg_typed_edges`, `L : Finset (Sym2 Node)` and swap-symmetry is definitional on the quotient, so `hL_symm` is gone — but the symmetric `Or` in $\Phi_L$ is kept. It is *semantically* faithful to the LN's "bifurcation between $\underline{v}$ and $\overline{v}$" phrasing (an undirected concept at the LN level), and downstream consumers (`claim_3_16`–`3_19`) may want a symmetric $\Phi_L$ for their own purposes. Dropping the `Or` here would be a gratuitous deviation from the original; the refactor's principle is "port mechanically, preserve LN-level semantics", not "trim every redundancy".

**$\Phi_L$ reuses `Walk.IsBifurcation` from `def_3_4` verbatim.** The LN's clause (iv) says "bifurcation in the sense of `def_3_4` item~vi"; the addition `[bifurcation_index_boundary_excludes_natural_cases]` pins this to the previously formalized notion (not the literal displayed index range). `Walk.IsBifurcation` already encodes the LN's item~vi (the $u \neq v$ clause, end-nodes-appear-exactly-once, the split-index left-arm/hinge/right-arm structure, and the clause-(e) end-node arrowhead constraint). All three boundary cases of the addition — $n = 1$ direct bidirected edge, $n = 2, k = 1$ Y-fork, $n = 2, k = n$ mirror Y — are admitted.

**Asymmetry between $E^{\sm W}$ and $L^{\sm W}$ preserved exactly.** Per the addition `[self_cycle_asymmetry_between_directed_and_bidirected]`: $E^{\sm W}$'s filter carries *no* `e.1 ≠ e.2` conjunct (self-cycle admission is controlled by $\Phi_E$ alone, including length-$1$ direct self-edges of $G.E$ that survive $V \setminus W$), while $L^{\sm W}$'s ordered-pair filter carries an *explicit* `e.1 ≠ e.2` as its first conjunct — the load-bearing discharge of `hL_irrefl` (the post-refactor `¬ s.IsDiag` form) and the primary mechanism excluding bidirected self-edges. `Walk.IsBifurcation`'s implicit $u \neq v$ clause is a secondary reinforcement, not the primary mechanism. The `Sym2.mk_isDiag_iff` lemma pulls the diagonal predicate back through the image to the underlying `e.1 = e.2`, contradicting the filter conjunct.

**Classical decidability and the consequent `noncomputable` annotation.** $\Phi_E$ and $\Phi_L$ are existentials over the `Walk G u v` inductive (whose length ranges freely up to the underlying CDMG's vertex count) and are not constructively decidable without a separate reachability or cycle-bound argument. `Finset.filter` requires `DecidablePred`, supplied by `Classical.propDecidable`-backed per-element instances; `noncomputable` propagates to `marginalize` in turn. Per-element instances (rather than a single `DecidablePred`) let typeclass resolution unify `u := e.1`, `v := e.2` for each concrete `e : Node × Node` flowing into the filter. Two separate instances (one each for $\Phi_E$ and $\Phi_L$) keep resolution decoupled across the two edge channels.

**The output is built with `where`-syntax and named fields, not the anonymous constructor `⟨ … ⟩`.** The post-refactor `CDMG` structure has eight fields, four of data and four of proof (`hJV_disj`, `hE_subset`, `hL_subset`, `hL_irrefl` — `hL_symm` is gone since `L : Finset (Sym2 Node)` is symmetric by quotient construction). An anonymous positional list would interleave data and proofs and obscure the correspondence with `def_3_1`; named-field syntax keeps each obligation labelled and adjacent to the data it constrains. The four private lemmas `marginalize_hJV_disj`, `marginalize_hE_subset`, `marginalize_hL_subset`, `marginalize_hL_irrefl` live outside the def body so the rendered statement reads as pure data plus named-lemma references — the convention shared by every chapter-3 CDMG operator.

**`hL_subset` is phrased over `Sym2.Mem`, not by destructuring `s = s(v₁, v₂)`.** The post-refactor `CDMG` axiom quantifies unordered-pair membership via `v ∈ s` rather than over an ordered pair. The proof unfolds `Finset.mem_image` to extract the pre-image `e : Node × Node` with `s = s(e.1, e.2)`, peels the filter via `Finset.mem_filter` and `Finset.mem_product` to obtain `e.1 ∈ G.V \ W ∧ e.2 ∈ G.V \ W`, then case-splits `Sym2.mem_iff.mp hv` into the two `v = e.1` / `v = e.2` branches.

**Argument order `(G) (W) (hW)` enables dot-notation.** Matches every other chapter-3 CDMG operator (`G.hardInterventionOn`, `G.nodeSplittingOn`, `G.nodeSplittingHard`, `G.extendingCDMGsWith`); $W$ precedes $hW$ so the call site reads left-to-right like the LN's "let $W \subseteq V$ be a subset".
